title: TTC: Digital Wayfinding & Information Architecture
subtitle: Conducted an IA audit and WCAG/AODA accessibility review, ran moderated usability tests, and proposed prioritized improvements to navigation, contrast, and content structure.
methods: IA audit, WCAG/AODA review, moderated usability testing, annotated wireframes
description: An information architecture audit and WCAG/AODA accessibility review of the Toronto Transit Commission's website, validated through moderated usability tests and translated into prioritized navigation, contrast, and content-structure improvements.
bullets: Conducted an information architecture audit and WCAG/AODA accessibility review to identify navigation and readability barriers.
Ran moderated usability tests to validate pain points across key journeys and quantify impact.
Proposed prioritized improvements to button hierarchy, color contrast, and content structure, and produced annotated wireframes and remediation recommendations for accessibility compliance.
challenge: Public transit riders often need information quickly — Is my train delayed? Which route should I take? How much will the trip cost? The TTC website is designed to answer these questions but finding those answers isn't always easy.
solution: Through usability evaluation and research-driven design improvements, restructured the IA to prioritize primary rider tasks over internal logic.
impact: Significantly reduced task completion time for common user journeys including trip planning and service alert lookup. Validated through usability testing with TTC riders.

sections.title: Context & Goal
sections.summary: Identifying usability gaps in a public-sector digital experience
sections.body: Public transit riders often need information quickly: Is my train delayed? Which route should I take? How much will the trip cost? The Toronto Transit Commission website is designed to answer these questions — but finding those answers isn't always easy. While the platform offers essential services like trip planning, fare details, and service alerts, users frequently encounter difficulties navigating the site and locating the information they need. This project began with a simple question: Why does such a critical service still feel difficult to use?

sections.problem: Users struggled to locate critical information quickly. Navigation labels did not align with user expectations, and key rider tasks were buried under secondary content — a misalignment between the site's structure and rider mental models.
sections.solution: Restructured the IA to prioritize primary rider tasks. Mapped user goals to content categories, simplified navigation hierarchy, and revised labels to match user language — shifting from organization-based to task-based navigation.

sections.title: Research & Evaluation
sections.summary: Evaluating the existing experience to uncover usability issues
sections.body: The project began with an evaluation of the current TTC website to understand where and why users encountered friction. Research activities included:
bullets: UX audit of the existing website
Task-based usability testing with riders
Observation of navigation behavior and task completion challenges
sections.body2: This phase focused on identifying patterns rather than isolated issues.

sections.caption: Usability testing with frequent and first-time TTC users
Header IA — before restructuring
Revised navigation hierarchy — task-based IA structure
New IA — task-based navigation

sections.title: Low-Fi Prototyping
sections.summary: Testing structure and flow before visual refinement
sections.body: Low-fidelity wireframes were created to validate the revised IA and navigation flow without introducing visual bias. The focus at this stage was:
bullets: Navigation clarity
Page structure
Task completion path
sections.body2: This allowed early feedback on structure before moving to detailed design.

sections.caption: User flow — Task 2
User flow — Task 3
Mid-fidelity design iterations

sections.title: Usability Testing & Iteration
sections.summary: Validating design decisions through user feedback
sections.body: The low-fi prototypes were tested with 5 users to assess whether the new structure improved clarity and usability. Feedback indicated:
bullets: Improved understanding of where to find information
Reduced navigation confusion
More predictable task flows

sections.caption: Fare & Passes design comparison

sections.title: Final Design
sections.summary: Refining the interface while preserving validated structure
sections.body: After validating the structure, the design was refined into higher-fidelity screens that maintained the tested IA while improving visual hierarchy and readability. Final designs aimed to:
bullets: Reinforce task prioritization
Improve scannability
Support accessibility considerations

sections.caption: Final high-fidelity design

sections.title: Outcome
sections.summary: Improved clarity and task efficiency in key rider flows
sections.body: The redesigned experience resulted in:
bullets: Clearer navigation structure
Faster access to key information
Greater user confidence when completing tasks
sections.body2: While this was a conceptual redesign, testing indicated meaningful improvements in usability and comprehension.

sections.caption: Outcome — before and after comparison

sections.title: Reflection
sections.summary: Designing for public-sector systems requires clarity, restraint, and validation
sections.body: This project reinforced the importance of:
bullets: Grounding design decisions in user research
Letting information architecture drive interface design
Iterating based on real user feedback rather than assumptions
sections.body2: While this was a conceptual redesign, testing indicated meaningful improvements in usability and comprehension.

title: Freshii: Omnichannel Research & Usability Optimization
subtitle: Planned and moderated remote usability tests using think-aloud protocols, synthesized findings, and translated them into prioritized UX recommendations to streamline the omnichannel customer experience.
methods: Moderated think-aloud testing, qualitative synthesis, prioritized UX recommendations
description: Planned and moderated remote usability tests of Freshii's ordering and checkout flows using think-aloud protocols, then synthesized findings into prioritized UX recommendations and design changes to reduce task errors and streamline the omnichannel customer experience.
bullets: Planned and moderated remote usability tests using think-aloud protocols to surface critical usability errors across ordering and checkout flows.
Led qualitative synthesis of test sessions to identify root causes of user confusion and error patterns.
Translated findings into prioritized UX recommendations and design changes to reduce task errors and streamline the omnichannel customer experience.
challenge: The Freshii mobile app's customization and checkout flows contained friction points that were leading to cart abandonment and negative user sentiment.
solution: Defined task scenarios and success criteria, moderated remote usability sessions, captured detailed behavioral notes, and authored the final report with actionable recommendations.
impact: Identified recurring usability issues across account creation, location discovery, rewards redemption, and ordering flows.

sections.title: Executive Summary
sections.summary: Focus: task success, clarity, and confidence in ordering
bullets: Focus: task success, clarity, and confidence in ordering
Methods: moderated testing and IA validation
Output: prioritized findings and recommendations
sections.body2: I defined task scenarios and success criteria, moderated remote usability sessions, captured detailed behavioral notes, synthesized findings into themes, and authored the final research report with clear, actionable recommendations.

sections.caption: Study overview and task framework
Research methodology and session planning overview

sections.title: Method & Approach
sections.summary: Combined task-based testing with IA validation to connect observed issues to structural causes
sections.body: Sessions followed a think-aloud protocol with post-task questions. Card sorting was used to validate navigation labels and grouping, helping explain why users struggled rather than only where they failed.

sections.caption: Think-aloud session approach and note-taking framework

sections.title: Tasks Evaluated
sections.summary: Four scenarios covering the full ordering journey
sections.body: The study evaluated four tasks:
bullets: Create an account
View rewards
Find the nearest store location
Order a custom bowl

sections.caption: Task scenario framework

sections.problem: Account creation is highly fragmented across multiple screens, requiring users to disclose personal information (ZIP code, birthday) early in the flow, with no option to skip. The lack of progress visibility and forced data entry reduces user control and increases drop-off risk.
sections.solution: Introduce progress indicators and clear completion feedback in multi-step flows. Allow optional inputs and clearer exit points during onboarding to reduce perceived commitment.

sections.caption: Scenario 1 — Account creation friction analysis
Scenario 2 — Location discovery issues: inconsistent iconography and unclear system states
Scenario 3 — Rewards buried deep in checkout flow
Scenario 4 — Custom bowl ordering: rigid linear flow with unclear requirements

sections.title: Outcome & Recommendations
sections.summary: Improving clarity, structure, and flexibility without redesigning the entire system
sections.body: Key recommendations:
bullets: Introduce progress indicators and clear completion feedback in multi-step flows
Improve categorization and labeling in rewards and menu sections
Allow optional inputs and clearer exit points during ordering

sections.caption: Prioritized recommendations report

sections.title: Reflection
sections.summary: Translating qualitative research into focused, actionable UX insights
bullets: This project strengthened my experience moderating usability tests and synthesizing qualitative data into clear findings.
It reinforced the importance of system feedback, information hierarchy, and user control in task-based mobile experiences.

sections.summary: Additional research artifacts available upon request
bullets: Affinity maps
Interview notes

title: Kijiji — Trust & Safety System
subtitle: Designing a comprehensive trust framework to reduce fraud and increase buyer confidence in peer-to-peer marketplace transactions.
methods: User research, journey mapping, system design, prototyping
description: A systems-level trust and safety framework designed to reduce fraud and improve buyer confidence on Kijiji's peer-to-peer marketplace.
challenge: Kijiji users frequently encounter scams, fraudulent listings, and safety concerns. Users hesitate when profiles are anonymous and listing quality is inconsistent.
solution: Worked backwards from user risk moments to define principles, then mapped flows and trust signals into the UI.
impact: Proposed a comprehensive trust framework with three core pillars — contextual verification, structured listings, and AI safety layer.

sections.title: Context & Problem
sections.summary: In P2P marketplaces, users need reassurance early because the cost of being wrong is high
bullets: Kijiji, as a peer-to-peer marketplace, faces ongoing challenges with trust and safety.
Users frequently encounter scams, fraudulent listings, and safety concerns when meeting strangers for transactions.
Users hesitate when profiles are anonymous and listing quality is inconsistent.
Trust must be visible in browse, listing view, and chat — not only after contact.

sections.caption: Trust journey — high-risk moments from browse to meetup

sections.title: Scope & My Role
sections.summary: Owned the system logic, flow mapping, and translated insights into a cohesive trust UI concept
bullets: Role: UX designer (systems + IA/flows + UI concept)
Deliverables: trust pillar framework, user flows, key screens, expected impact metrics (proposed)
Collaboration: team project; I focused on structure, decision points, and how trust is communicated

sections.title: Approach
sections.summary: Worked backwards from user risk moments to define principles, then mapped flows and trust signals
bullets: Identify high-risk moments (browse → contact → meetup)
Define trust pillars and placement rules
Design flows + UI touchpoints where trust matters most

sections.caption: Three key design decisions shaping the trust system

sections.problem: Single-point verification creates a false sense of security and blocks legitimate sellers. Users encounter suspicious listings, anonymous profiles, and no in-context safety signals during browse and contact flow.
sections.solution: Layered trust signals across identity, listings, and behavior. Contextual identity verification triggered by high-risk actions. Listing quality enforced before publishing. AI acts as safety net, not gatekeeper.

sections.caption: Decision 1 — Contextual identity verification: triggered via settings or high-risk actions
Decision 2 — Listing quality enforced before publishing: category-specific required fields
Decision 3 — AI safety layer: scans content post-publish, flags suspicious listings
Kijiji trust & safety system — full framework overview

sections.title: Recommendations
sections.summary: Introduce contextual verification + structured listings + AI-assisted warnings
bullets: Contextual verification triggered by high-risk categories/price thresholds
Pre-publish listing structure (required specs, completeness checks)
AI safety layer: warn/hold suspicious content post-publish with transparent messaging

sections.title: Reflection
sections.summary: Designing trust at scale requires balancing safety, accessibility, and user autonomy
bullets: Trust must be visible at decision points
Over-verification risks excluding valid users
Platform responsibility extends beyond digital interactions
sections.body2: This project reinforced my interest in designing systems where UX decisions directly affect real-world safety.

title: BeaverBuild — Financial Literacy for Newcomers
subtitle: Researching why newcomer Ontario college students can't act on the financial information that already exists — and designing a paced, jargon-free learning path to close that gap.
users: Newcomer Ontario college students (international, low-income, recent immigrants)
methods: Interviews, UX audit, gap analysis, affinity mapping, ideation, high-fi prototyping, moderated usability testing
description: A team UX research and product-design project for a web platform helping newcomer Ontario college students understand and navigate Canadian finances — banking, credit, budgeting, and OSAP — through a paced, milestone-based learning experience.
challenge: Newcomer college students arrive in Ontario facing an immediate, high-stakes crash course in a financial system they've never touched. The information exists — government sites, college workshops, fintech apps — but none of it was built for them.
solution: We pivoted from a content aggregator to a paced, milestone-based learning journey: Day 1 → Week 1 → Month 1 → Month 6. Newcomer-first language, single-task screens, and a guided roadmap that surfaces only what matters at each moment.
impact: 5-person Humber UXD team. 4 newcomer interviews across 3 Ontario colleges. 5-participant moderated usability test produced a prioritized list of clarity, guidance, engagement, efficiency, and visual-design improvements that were applied across sign-up, roadmap, knowledge hub, community, and tools.

sections.title: Context
sections.summary: The information exists. Acting on it doesn't.
sections.body: Newcomer students arriving at Ontario colleges face an immediate, high-stakes crash course in a financial system they have never touched. They are not failing because the information isn't out there — government sites, college workshops, and fintech apps are everywhere. They are failing because none of those resources were built for them.
sections.body2: BeaverBuild is a team project from Humber's UX Design program (UXD-5213, Group 2). I led research synthesis, contributed to ideation, and co-designed the high-fidelity prototype that was tested with newcomer students.

sections.title: Problem vs. Design Goal
sections.problem: Resources are scattered across government portals, college emails, and bank websites with no clear starting point. All of them assume baseline understanding of Canadian credit, taxes, and banking terminology. Students spend their first 6–12 months on debit only out of fear, missing the most critical credit-building window — and quietly disengage from financial topics entirely. No product treats newcomer students as a primary audience.
sections.solution: A single, paced entry point that meets students where they are on Day 1. Jargon-free guidance that builds confidence before complexity. A structured first-year financial roadmap — not a static information dump. Relevant, credible content filtered for the newcomer context. A resource students would actually open, not archive.

sections.title: Four-phase process
sections.summary: From scattered signals to a clear design direction.
sections.body: We ran secondary and primary research in parallel — evaluating existing tools for their UX assumptions, then speaking directly with newcomer students about their lived financial experience. Synthesis connected what the tools do with what students actually need.
bullets: 01 · Discover — Secondary research & UX audit. Evaluated Canada.ca, Humber/Seneca/Sheridan portals, and KOHO, YNAB, Wealthsimple. Documented both what each offers and what it assumes users already know.

02 · Define — Interviews with 4 newcomer students. One-on-one semi-structured interviews with low-income international students from three Ontario colleges. Explored lived financial experiences, emotional responses, and real coping behaviours.

03 · Develop — Synthesis & gap analysis. Cross-referenced what tools offer vs. what students need. Three concrete gaps emerged: scattered resources, no newcomer-specific targeting, and text-heavy formats that reward prior knowledge.

04 · Deliver — Concept sketching → mid-fi → high-fi prototype. A milestone-based experience: Day 1 → Week 1 → Month 1 → Month 6. Each stage introduces only the financial concept relevant to that moment in a student's first year.

sections.title: Three patterns, heard from every participant
sections.summary: Four interviews. Three insights that cut across all of them.
sections.body: We interviewed four low-income international students — one each from Humber, Seneca, and Sheridan colleges — about their first months managing money in Canada. Three consistent patterns came up in every conversation.
bullets: Delayed financial confidence — "Almost everything felt unfamiliar at the beginning." Students limited themselves to debit only for the first 6–12 months — not from choice, but from fear of the unknown. Low awareness of credit, taxes, and banking terminology meant they missed the most critical window to build a financial foundation.

Information overload & low visibility — "I didn't even know that such an assessment existed." College emails went unread. Government websites felt cluttered and were avoided early on. Students often didn't know what they didn't know — existing resources couldn't reach them because they weren't surfaced at the right moment or in a digestible form.

Reactive, not proactive — "I mostly just check the transaction records in my bank app." Without structured first-year guidance, students managed money reactively — checking balances after the fact rather than planning ahead. Unexpected expenses were consistently hard to anticipate, and no budgeting tools were in use.

sections.title: The pivot moment
sections.summary: We almost built the wrong thing.
sections.body: Early ideation pulled us toward a resource aggregator — essentially a better-organized version of Canada.ca. It made logical sense: the problem was scattered information, so consolidate it. But the interviews surfaced something deeper than information overload — students lacked a mental model for Canadian finance. They didn't need more content organized more cleanly; they needed someone to tell them what matters on Day 1, what to learn in Month 1, and why it matters at all.
sections.body2: This was uncomfortable to admit midway through ideation. We had already invested time mapping content architecture for an aggregator model. But the data was clear: another well-organized list of links wouldn't move the needle. The emotional barrier — feeling lost, not just uninformed — required a fundamentally different approach. We shifted from a content hub to a paced milestone journey: a guide, not a library.

sections.title: Key design decisions
sections.summary: Every decision traced back to a research finding.
bullets: Milestone-based timeline (Day 1 → Month 6) instead of a topic library — temporal anchoring reduces cognitive load by surfacing only what's relevant to the student's current moment. Directly addresses the "reactive, not proactive" pattern.

Newcomer-first language — no financial jargon without an immediate plain-language explanation. Every existing tool we audited assumed baseline financial literacy; participants avoided resources that felt like tests of knowledge they didn't have.

Mobile-first, single-task screens rather than information-dense pages — college and government portals were described as "cluttered" and were actively avoided. One concept per screen reduces decision fatigue.
Proactive push at enrollment — not buried in a college portal sidebar. The tool has to reach students before they develop avoidance habits, not after.

Progress-visible design ("Month 1 of 6" rather than endless scroll) — financial learning felt boundless and therefore paralyzing. Visible progress creates a sense of completion and lowers the emotional cost of engaging.

sections.title: Prototype features
sections.summary: Four product surfaces, each addressing a specific research gap.
sections.body: The high-fidelity prototype is built around four complementary surfaces — each one mapped to a barrier we heard in interviews.
bullets: Guided learning modules — A first-year financial roadmap broken into four milestones (Financial Survival Guide → Essential Setup → Building Roots → Long-term Planning). Auto-saved progress, plain-language module summaries, and a visible "X of 4 complete" bar so the journey feels finite.

Knowledge Hub — A curated library of plain-language videos, guides, and articles. Filtered for the newcomer context and tagged so students can find a topic without already knowing the Canadian terminology for it.
Community Hub — Peer-to-peer questions, shared journeys, and points for posting. An admin presence and trust signals encourage participation from students who would otherwise lurk.
Financial tools — Lightweight calculators (budget planner, savings tracker) that surface contextual tips when a user has extra budget left over, instead of waiting for the user to know what to ask.

sections.title: A first-year financial milestone guide
sections.summary: Six milestones, unlocked over time. One concept per screen.
sections.body: The mobile-first concept walks newcomer students through six financial milestones in their first year in Canada. Each milestone is unlocked over time, introducing one concept at a time, with plain language and a single recommended action.

sections.caption: Milestone Map — Students see the full journey at a glance (Day 1 → Week 1 → Month 1 → Month 6), making financial learning feel finite and achievable.
Day 1 — Financial Survival Guide. Only the essentials: open an account, understand debit vs. credit, get your SIN.
Month 1 — Credit Introduction. Credit explained from scratch: why it matters in Canada, how to start building it safely.
Budget Awareness — Visual income-to-expense breakdown without the overwhelm of full budgeting categories.
Wireframe Flow — Full journey from landing through all six milestones, showing navigation logic and content pacing decisions.

sections.title: Usability testing
sections.summary: Five participants, five tasks, one prioritized backlog.
sections.body: We ran moderated, task-based usability tests with five international students in Canada — 15–20 minutes per session, think-aloud protocol. Tasks: sign up, use the financial roadmap, find an article, explore the community, use a tool. We measured task completion, time on task, observations, participant quotes, and post-task ratings.
bullets: Sign up — Users were unsure where to sign up. Multiple entry points caused confusion and the sign-up was not visually emphasized.
Financial Roadmap — Users were confused by stage names and terminology. Lack of instruction and context made progression unclear.
Knowledge Hub — Some labels and categories (notably "My Library") were unclear; content didn't always match user expectations and articles needed more structure.
Community — Users were unsure if others would actively post. Low engagement signals and no clear motivation to participate.
Tools — No tips after a budget was entered; users wanted simple suggestions for what to do with leftover budget.
sections.body2: Findings were tagged across five themes — clarity, guidance, engagement & trust, efficiency, and visual design — so iterations could be prioritized rather than addressed ad-hoc.

sections.title: Before → After iterations
sections.summary: What we changed, and why.
bullets: Sign up — Added a time concept to the financial plan, made the register button bigger and added a Register CTA on the guest homepage, replaced stylized illustrations with real human imagery, and added a clear register progress flow.
Roadmap — Added inline instructions, a short hero explanation, and revealed more information in the financial roadmap section so users no longer needed to scroll to discover the larger map.
Roadmap modules — Added descriptions under each module (and inside each module) so users understood the purpose before clicking in.
Knowledge Hub — Renamed "My Library" and adjusted category labels to match user mental models.
Knowledge Hub articles — Broke long-form articles into shorter sections with clear headings to better support comprehension.
Community — Added a search function, an admin presence that posts and replies, and a visible points reward to encourage participation.
Tools — Added contextual tips in the budget planner (e.g. simple suggestions when there's extra budget left).

sections.title: Marketing & touchpoints
sections.summary: Reaching students before avoidance habits set in.
sections.body: Because students reported ignoring school emails and not knowing resources existed until it was too late, the platform's reach has to begin before they're sitting at a laptop. We mapped six newcomer touchpoints and a marketing material for each.
bullets: School acceptance email — Embedded checklist linking to the onboarding hub.
Airport arrival — Booklets and 3D-printed luggage tags with a QR code to the platform.
Service & Canada Bank visits — Standees and sign holders next to high-anxiety in-branch tasks.
On-campus orientation — Postcards students can send home that double as platform awareness.
Daily financial activities — Personal roadmap and flyers placed where decisions actually happen.
Social media & peer networks — Knowledge hub teasers, financial tools, and the community hub used as conversation starters.
sections.body2: Each material maps back to a specific pain point — scattered information, anxiety, heavy text, confusion over financial terms, or low motivation — so the marketing doesn't just create awareness, it sets expectations for what the product will solve.

sections.title: Impact & reflection
sections.summary: What this research revealed about designing for newcomers.
bullets: 4 low-income international students interviewed across 3 Ontario colleges (Humber, Seneca, Sheridan).
3 critical gaps identified where no existing tool met student needs.
0 existing products that treat newcomer students as a primary design audience.
5-participant moderated usability test produced a 5-theme prioritization (clarity, guidance, engagement & trust, efficiency, visual design) applied across the high-fi prototype.
sections.body2: Lessons learned: (1) Cognitive load is an equity issue — when tools assume financial literacy, they don't just create friction, they exclude. (2) Timing matters as much as content — the right information at the wrong time doesn't help. (3) Don't confuse information access with information usability — Canada.ca is comprehensive; students still couldn't use it. (4) Inclusive design starts with who's in the room during research — "newcomer" is broad, and future testing should include recent permanent residents, refugees, and domestic students from low-income backgrounds to validate whether the milestone model holds across different newcomer experiences.

sections.title: What's next
sections.summary: Prototyping & validation.
sections.body: This case study represents Phase 2 of the project — research synthesis and early ideation through to a high-fi prototype. The next phase moves into broader interactive testing, incorporating Humber's Idea Lab Audio & Visual resources to validate the pacing model with a wider group of newcomer students.
sections.body2: Key questions for the next round: Does the milestone structure feel intuitive without coaching? Does plain-language content reduce anxiety, or does it feel too simplified? And — critically — will students actually open it on Day 1? Accessibility note: the next prototype phase treats WCAG 2.1 AA as a baseline, not a bonus — plain language, ≥4.5:1 contrast for body text, multilingual support, and screen-reader compatibility are non-negotiable for users with varying English literacy levels.

title: Best Buy — Competitive UX Analysis
subtitle: A competitive UX analysis examining how retail experience patterns shape trust, decision-making, and purchase confidence.
methods: Heuristic evaluation, user flow mapping, IA analysis, journey mapping, service blueprint
description: A competitive UX analysis examining Best Buy alongside comparable electronics retailers to identify recurring UX patterns, strengths, and gaps across discovery, comparison, and checkout experiences.
challenge: Electronics purchases involve high financial risk, delivery concerns, and return anxiety. Users require clear system feedback and strong trust signals throughout the decision-making process.
solution: Evaluated structure, flow, and surface cues across the end-to-end purchase journey using heuristic evaluation, user flow mapping, IA analysis, and journey mapping.
impact: Produced a detailed analysis report with specific UX improvements across web, mobile, and in-store digital interactions.

sections.title: Context
sections.summary: Buying expensive electronics amplifies users' need for reassurance before commitment
sections.body: Electronics purchases involve high financial risk and uncertainty around delivery and returns. Users rely on clear product information and strong trust signals before committing to a purchase.
sections.body2: Primary user: a budget-conscious buyer, purchasing electronics online with home delivery, who prioritizes reliability, clarity, and risk reduction.

sections.title: Key Findings
sections.summary: Clarity builds trust — but complexity still appears at critical decision points
bullets: Finding 1 — Strong product-driven surface design: clear visual hierarchy and consistent labeling across browsing
Finding 2 — Complex flows during sign-in and checkout: branching paths increase cognitive load
Finding 3 — Policy and delivery reassurance comes late: surfaced after users have already invested effort in comparison

sections.caption: User Flow 1 — Sign-in journey
User Flow 2 — Product purchase journey

sections.title: Service Blueprint
sections.summary: Mapping how user experience is supported across touchpoints
sections.body: This service blueprint maps how the user experience is supported across different touchpoints, including digital platforms, physical interactions, and internal operational processes.

sections.caption: Service Blueprint — online experience & employee touchpoints
Service Blueprint — physical store experience
Service Blueprint — combined online & in-store touchpoints
Service Blueprint — hybrid experience
Service Blueprint — B2B

sections.problem: Complex flows during sign-in and checkout introduce branching paths that increase cognitive load. Policy and delivery reassurance comes late — often surfaced after users have already invested effort in comparison. Category trees are overwhelmingly deep with limited progressive disclosure for novice users.
sections.solution: Surface trust signals and return/delivery policies earlier in the decision flow. Reduce branching paths in authentication. Use progressive disclosure to simplify deep category navigation for users with narrow goals.

sections.title: Information Architecture
sections.summary: A robust IA supports scale, but depth creates friction
sections.body: What works:
bullets: Clear top-level categories
Consistent labeling
Strong separation between shopping and support
bullets2: Limited progressive disclosure for novice users

sections.caption: IA — what works: clear top-level categories, consistent labeling
IA — where it struggles: overwhelming depth, limited progressive disclosure

sections.title: Visual Design & Trust
sections.summary: Color, hierarchy, and contrast reinforce credibility and action
bullets: Best Buy's visual system uses blue as a dominant color to convey trust, technology, and authority. Yellow accents function as high-visibility action triggers, especially in pricing and promotions.
Together, these choices guide users toward confident action while maintaining brand recognition.

sections.caption: Visual design analysis — homepage
Visual design analysis — product listing
Visual design analysis — product detail
Visual design analysis — checkout flow
Competitive analysis — mobile product browsing experience
Competitive analysis — search and filtering patterns
Competitive analysis — product comparison view
Competitive analysis — trust and reassurance signals
Competitive analysis — checkout experience patterns
Competitive analysis — category navigation structure
Competitive analysis — mobile homepage layout
Competitive analysis — product detail page patterns
Competitive analysis — cart and pre-checkout experience
Competitive analysis — delivery and pickup options
Competitive analysis — returns and support flows
Competitive analysis — loyalty and rewards integration
Competitor brand analysis — visual identity comparison
Competitor brand analysis — logo and mark study

sections.title: Opportunities
sections.summary: Surface reassurance earlier to reduce hesitation
bullets: Introduce clearer "What's included" summaries for refurbished products
Surface delivery and return information earlier in comparison views
Reduce perceived checkout complexity through clearer progress feedback

sections.title: Reflection
sections.summary: Trust is cumulative and easily disrupted
bullets: This analysis reinforced that trust isn't created by a single screen or policy. It's built through consistent clarity, predictable structure, and timely reassurance across the entire journey.
Designing for high-value purchases means reducing uncertainty before users feel it.

title: Duolingo — Behavior & Motivation Research
subtitle: A moderated UX research study uncovering motivation breakdowns and learning flow friction in Duolingo's mobile experience.
methods: Moderated usability testing, think-aloud protocol, task analysis, card sorting
description: Behavioral UX research examining how Duolingo's gamification mechanics influence daily engagement patterns and long-term learning retention.
challenge: Users lose motivation and clarity during learning sessions despite Duolingo's strong gamification. The research aimed to identify moments where motivation drops and learning flow is disrupted.
solution: Led the research planning and execution: defining task scenarios, moderating sessions, capturing qualitative data, and synthesizing findings.
impact: Identified 3 key UX gaps: abstract progress feedback, streak pressure creating anxiety, and missing post-session guidance.

sections.title: Context & Goal
sections.summary: Understanding where and why learning momentum breaks during real user sessions
bullets: Problem: Users lose motivation and clarity during learning sessions despite Duolingo's strong gamification
Approach: Moderated usability testing focused on task flow & feedback
Outcome: Identified 3 key UX gaps with concrete improvement opportunities
sections.body2: This study focused on identifying where learning motivation breaks during real usage and how feedback and flow affect engagement. Through moderated usability testing, I uncovered recurring friction points and translated them into clear UX opportunity areas to improve learning continuity.

sections.caption: Research framework and study overview
Improvement recommendation on user flow
Research planning and participant session overview

sections.title: Research Approach
sections.summary: Moderated usability testing focused on task flow, feedback, and motivation
sections.body: I led the research planning and execution, including defining task scenarios, moderating sessions, capturing qualitative data, and synthesizing findings. Key methods:
bullets: Moderated usability testing
Think-aloud protocol
Task scenarios: starting lessons, reviewing progress, continuing learning
Card sorting for research synthesis

sections.caption: Research method — moderated testing session structure

sections.title: Key Findings
sections.summary: Three recurring UX issues consistently disrupted learning motivation and flow
bullets: Finding 1 — Progress feedback feels abstract: Users understood they were 'learning' but struggled to grasp how much progress they had made or what goal they were working toward.
Finding 2 — Streak mechanics create pressure: Streaks initially motivated users but introduced anxiety after breaks, discouraging re-engagement.
Finding 3 — Session endings lack guidance: After completing a lesson, users were unsure what to do next, breaking learning momentum.

sections.caption: Finding 1 & 2 — Abstract progress and streak-induced anxiety
Finding 3 — Session endings with no guidance

sections.problem: Three recurring issues: abstract progress feedback, streak pressure creating anxiety after breaks, and session endings that leave users directionless — all disrupting learning continuity despite strong gamification design.
sections.solution: Make learning progress more tangible through short-term milestones. Reframe streak feedback to emphasize encouragement over pressure. Provide guided next steps at the end of every learning session.

sections.title: Opportunities
sections.summary: Research insights translated into actionable opportunity areas
bullets: Make learning progress more tangible through short-term milestones
Reframe streak feedback to emphasize encouragement over pressure
Provide guided next steps at the end of learning sessions

sections.caption: UX opportunity — reducing friction in new user onboarding
UX opportunity — enhancing transparency in the rewards system
UX opportunity — improving post-session guidance and continuity

sections.title: Reflection
sections.summary: Designing for learning requires emotional and motivational sensitivity
sections.body: This project reinforced that motivation is emotional, not purely functional. The timing and tone of feedback can significantly influence user engagement, and clear research insights are often more valuable than polished visual solutions.

sections.caption: Supporting research artifacts — research plans, collected data, synthesis documentation

title: Art & Creative — Selected Gallery
subtitle: A collection of illustration, art direction, photography, and motion work exploring the intersection of emotion and form.
users: Brand collaborators, creative agencies, personal projects
methods: Art direction, photography, illustration, motion, brand identity
description: Visual worlds, crafted by hand. A continuously growing body of personal and commissioned creative work.
challenge: Translating intuition and feeling into work that holds together as a single visual voice across very different mediums.
solution: An ongoing gallery of illustration, photography, art direction, brand identity, and motion — letting the through-lines (color, rhythm, composition) speak across pieces.
impact: A living archive of the creative practice that runs in parallel with the product work — used as both a personal reference and a way to invite collaborators into the broader visual world.

sections.title: Visual worlds, crafted by hand
sections.summary: A collection of illustration, art direction, photography, and motion work
sections.body: This is an ongoing collection of personal and commissioned work spanning art direction, illustration, photography, brand identity, and motion. Each piece is an exploration into how form, color, and rhythm can carry feeling — moving between stillness and energy, structure and improvisation.

sections.problem: Creative work is often filed away after delivery — separated from the thinking, the references, and the moments of decision that gave it shape.
sections.solution: This gallery brings the work back together as one continuous body, letting the through-lines (color sensibility, spatial intuition, narrative pacing) speak for themselves across mediums.

sections.title: Reflection
sections.summary: Why this work sits next to the product design practice
sections.body: Working across art and product design isn't a contradiction — both ask the same questions about how something should feel and what it should make a person notice. The gallery is a reminder that craft is craft, regardless of where it ends up living.
